literal: being, existing; present participle of √as 'to be'
invariant_definition: Being, that-which-is. In Vedānta technical usage *sat* names the highest mode of reality — that which is real in the most demanding sense (uncaused, eternal, unsublatable). *Brahman* is *sat* on every Vedāntic account. The contrast is with *asat* (non-existent: the son of a barren woman) and (in Advaita) with *mithyā* (sublatable, neither *sat* nor *asat*). Schools agree on *brahman*'s being *sat*; they differ on whether the world is *sat* (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Dvaita, Vaiṣṇava bhedābheda — yes, the world is *satya* even if dependent) or *mithyā* (Advaita).

per_school.definition: Only *brahman* is *sat* in the strict sense (*sad-eva idam agra āsīt*, Chāndogya 6.2.1). The world is *mithyā* — not *sat*, not *asat*. *Sat* is one of the three constitutive aspects of *brahman*: *sat-cit-ānanda*.

*Brahman* is *sat* in the highest sense; *cit* and *acit* are *sat* in the dependent sense. The world is *satya* (real, not *mithyā*); the demand of Advaita that real-status requires *brahman*-mode existence is rejected.

translator_note: 'Being' or 'existence' are acceptable but English collapses *sat* (the real-substantial), *satya* (truth/the-true), and *vidyamāna* (occurring) into one word. The Sanskrit distinguishes.
